title: Johnson & Johnson — Neuroscience — briefing Q1-2026
html:
J&J Neuroscience Quarterly Briefing — Q1 2026

Quarter: January–March 2026 Earnings reported: J&J April 14 | Axsome April 29 (pending) | Compass Pathways — no quarterly revenue (pre-commercial) Generated: 2026-04-27

Governing Thought

SPRAVATO continues strong growth (~$497M Q1, +55% YoY) but the competitive landscape fundamentally shifted: COMP360 psilocybin hit its second Phase 3 primary endpoint in February, received FDA rolling NDA review + Commissioner's National Priority Voucher in April, and now has a clear 2027 approval pathway. Simultaneously, Axsome's Auvelity is quietly building an MDD installed base that captures patients before they escalate to SPRAVATO. The psilocybin clock is ticking — J&J has ~12 months to maximize SPRAVATO's market position before COMP360 arrives.

1. J&J Financial Signals

Metric Q1 2026 Commentary
SPRAVATO quarterly ~$497M +55.2% YoY; annual run rate ~$2B
Growth moderation 67.8% (Q4) → 55.2% (Q1) First quarter of deceleration — seasonal or early plateau?
J&J total $24.1B 9.9% growth; raised outlook to $100-101B
CAPLYTA Building Adjunctive MDD gaining traction; neuroscience portfolio expanding
  • Growth moderation from 67.8% to 55.2% is notable — first time SPRAVATO growth has decelerated quarter-over-quarter
  • ~$497M quarterly puts SPRAVATO on track for ~$2B annually — a significant franchise
  • J&J framing neuroscience as "SPRAVATO + CAPLYTA" portfolio — diversifying the growth narrative beyond TRD

What J&J management said on the Q1 2026 call:

  1. "SPRAVATO continues strong growth trajectory" — management maintained a positive tone despite the deceleration from 67.8% to 55.2%. The "continues" framing is designed to normalize the growth rate moderation rather than flag it as a concern.
  2. Q1 seasonality cited — when asked about the deceleration, management noted "Q1 seasonality is typical in psychiatry." This is a legitimate observation (January-February often see lower psychiatric visits) but also a convenient explanation that avoids addressing competitive dynamics.
  3. "Full-year trajectory intact" — management explicitly reassured that the growth deceleration is temporary and the FY2026 outlook remains achievable. This language suggests internal models still show SPRAVATO growing at 50%+ for the full year.
  4. Real-world evidence emphasized in COMP360 context — when asked again about psilocybin competition, J&J returned to the "focused on building SPRAVATO's real-world evidence base" defense. This is the second consecutive quarter where J&J's COMP360 answer centers on SPRAVATO's track record rather than clinical differentiation.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts asked about growth moderation directly — the deceleration from 67.8% to 55.2% was flagged as a concern. J&J's seasonality explanation was accepted but with visible skepticism. If Q2 doesn't rebound above 60%, the "seasonal" defense will be challenged.
  2. COMP360 was raised for the second consecutive quarter — the psilocybin question is becoming a recurring feature of J&J earnings calls, signaling the Street is pricing in competitive risk. J&J's consistent "real-world evidence" response is becoming stale — analysts want a more specific competitive strategy.
  3. No analyst connected the growth deceleration to Auvelity or generic ketamine — the competitive analysis on the call remains focused on COMP360 (future threat) rather than Auvelity or generic ketamine (current threats). This suggests the Street may be overlooking the near-term competitive dynamics in favor of the headline psilocybin narrative.

2. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q1 2026

Company Brand Q1 2026 Key Signal
Compass Pathways COMP360 Pre-revenue Phase 3 #2 positive (Feb); rolling NDA + CNPV (Apr); 2027 approval pathway
Axsome Auvelity ~$100M (pending) Growing 80%+ in MDD augmentation; oral convenience
BMS Cobenfy ~$70M (pending) Schizophrenia (different indication but same prescriber base)
SPRAVATO ~$497M +55.2% YoY; first deceleration

Compass Pathways — COMP360 (Pre-Revenue, 2027 Approval Pathway)

Compass Pathways developments (Q1 2026 — not an earnings call; regulatory/clinical milestones):

  1. Second Phase 3 hit primary endpoint (February 2026): COMP006 showed -3.8 MADRS treatment difference (p<0.001); 39% of 25mg arm achieved ≥25% MADRS reduction at Week 6. This is the second positive Phase 3, following COMP005. Two positive pivotal trials dramatically increase approval probability.

  2. FDA rolling NDA review + CNPV (April 2026): FDA granted rolling NDA request AND Commissioner's National Priority Voucher — the combination signals regulatory enthusiasm. Rolling review means Compass can submit sections as they're ready (vs. waiting for complete package). CNPV adds 1-2 months faster review. NDA submission target: Q4 2026; potential approval H1 2027.

  3. COMP360's clinical profile directly challenges SPRAVATO's model:

    • Dosing: 1-2 supervised sessions vs. SPRAVATO's indefinite twice-monthly maintenance
    • Durability: Effects within 1 day, durability through 6 months vs. SPRAVATO's continuous dosing requirement
    • Session burden: 1-2 total sessions vs. dozens per year with SPRAVATO
    • Safety: Majority of AEs mild-moderate, resolve within 24 hours; no dissociative monitoring window needed
    • Reimbursement: UNDEFINED — this is COMP360's biggest commercial uncertainty. No CPT code, no reimbursement pathway, no payer experience.

  4. What COMP360 doesn't solve: Reimbursement uncertainty, infrastructure requirements (certified therapist + certified setting), limited commercial track record, potential scheduling restrictions (Schedule I vs. SPRAVATO's Schedule III).

Implication for J&J: COMP360 is the most significant competitive threat SPRAVATO has ever faced. The 1-2 session model vs. indefinite maintenance is a fundamentally different value proposition. J&J's defense: REMS infrastructure already built (3,000+ certified centers), insurance coverage established, physician familiarity. COMP360 must build all of this from scratch. But the durability narrative — "two sessions, six months of benefit" — is powerful if it holds in real-world practice.

Axsome Therapeutics — Auvelity (~$100M est., pending Apr 29)

Axsome Q1 2026 pending (April 29):

  1. Auvelity quietly building MDD market position: At $100M quarterly ($400M annual run rate), Auvelity (dextromethorphan-bupropion) is the fastest-growing oral MDD treatment. Its NMDA mechanism and oral formulation position it as the "step before SPRAVATO" — patients try Auvelity before escalating to intranasal esketamine.

  2. Auvelity's growth matters because it captures patients earlier: Every patient who responds to Auvelity is a patient who doesn't escalate to SPRAVATO. This isn't direct competitive displacement — it's funnel interception. If Auvelity works for a patient, SPRAVATO never enters the conversation.

  3. Axsome's expansion into TRD is the watch item: Axsome has discussed potential TRD-specific studies for Auvelity. If Auvelity demonstrates efficacy in treatment-resistant patients (currently off-label for TRD), it becomes a direct oral competitor to SPRAVATO's intranasal REMS model.

Implication for J&J: Auvelity is more dangerous than its revenue suggests because it intercepts patients upstream. J&J's counter: SPRAVATO is for the patients who've already failed — the treatment-resistant population that oral agents can't reach. But if Auvelity captures 20% of patients who would have been referred to SPRAVATO, that's ~$400M in addressable market reduction over time.

BMS — Cobenfy (~$70M est., pending Apr 24)

BMS Q1 2026 (April 24, if disclosed separately):

  1. Cobenfy (xanomeline-trospium) launched for schizophrenia: The first new schizophrenia mechanism in 30+ years. Not a SPRAVATO competitor by indication, but relevant because it targets the same psychiatrist prescriber base. If Cobenfy demands significant prescriber attention, it could reduce SPRAVATO SOV in psychiatrist offices.

  2. BMS's psychiatry investment signals market growth: BMS investing heavily in schizophrenia suggests the CNS/psychiatry market is attracting big pharma attention. This could indirectly benefit SPRAVATO by expanding the psychiatrist infrastructure and payer attention for CNS therapies.

Implication for J&J: Cobenfy is not a direct competitor but a share-of-voice competitor. Psychiatrists have limited appointment time; if Cobenfy dominates the conversation, SPRAVATO messaging gets crowded out.

Generic Ketamine Clinics (No earnings — market dynamics)

  1. 3,500+ clinics nationwide as of Q1 2026: The generic ketamine ecosystem continues expanding. Telehealth-enabled at-home ketamine protocols are growing fastest, bypassing the REMS-certified center requirement entirely.

  2. Price competition is real: Generic ketamine sessions cost $200-500 vs. SPRAVATO's $700-900 per session (plus REMS monitoring costs). For cost-sensitive patients, generic ketamine is the pragmatic alternative.

  3. Quality and safety are generic ketamine's weakness: Lack of standardization, variable dosing protocols, and no REMS monitoring create safety concerns. But payers and patients often prioritize cost over standardization.

Implication for J&J: Generic ketamine is the "good enough" threat — patients who can't afford SPRAVATO or can't access certified centers default to generic ketamine clinics. J&J's REMS is both a quality moat and an access barrier.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us

  1. SPRAVATO faces a three-horizon competitive threat:

    • Near-term (now): Generic ketamine (3,500+ clinics, price advantage) + Auvelity (upstream interception)
    • Medium-term (2027): COMP360 psilocybin (fundamentally different model — 1-2 sessions vs. indefinite maintenance)
    • Long-term (2028+): Aticaprant (J&J's own oral option) + next-gen psychedelics + TMS evolution

  2. COMP360's 2027 timeline creates a 12-month window for SPRAVATO to maximize position — J&J needs to: (a) lock in payer contracts before COMP360 disrupts the conversation, (b) expand certified center count while REMS infrastructure is a moat, (c) build real-world evidence that chronic maintenance works (COMP360's counter-narrative is "why maintain forever when you can do 2 sessions?")

  3. The growth deceleration (67.8% → 55.2%) deserves investigation — is it seasonal (Q1 typically slower in psychiatry), base effect, or the first signal of competitive pressure from generic ketamine/Auvelity? ZoomRx tracking can disambiguate.

3. Clinical/Regulatory

  • COMP360 Phase 3 #2 positive (Feb): -3.8 MADRS treatment difference; rolling NDA + CNPV (Apr)
  • Aticaprant (J&J) Phase 3 advancing: Kappa opioid antagonist for MDD — potential oral complement to SPRAVATO
  • SPRAVATO REMS unchanged: No relaxation of in-clinic monitoring requirement; at-home administration not on horizon
  • Accelerated TMS (Stanford SAINT): 5-day intensive protocol gaining insurance coverage — non-pharmacological alternative
  • MDMA regulatory setback (2024): FDA rejection creates psychedelic class uncertainty, but COMP360's CNPV suggests psilocybin is on a different regulatory track

4. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Evidence This Quarter
1. SPRAVATO's REMS is both moat and barrier CONFIRMED 3,000+ centers = infrastructure moat; but generic ketamine clinics bypass REMS entirely
2. COMP360 is the existential threat STRONGLY CONFIRMED Second Phase 3 positive, rolling NDA, CNPV — 2027 approval pathway real; 1-2 session model challenges infinite maintenance
3. Auvelity intercepts upstream STRENGTHENED ~$100M quarterly, growing 80%+; capturing MDD patients before they escalate to SPRAVATO
4. Growth deceleration needs diagnosis NEW 67.8% → 55.2% — first deceleration; seasonal? base effect? competitive pressure?
5. J&J portfolio defense (CAPLYTA + aticaprant) IN PROGRESS CAPLYTA building; aticaprant Phase 3; 3-product neuroscience portfolio if aticaprant succeeds

5. What to Watch Next Quarter (Q2 2026)

  1. SPRAVATO Q2 growth — does it rebound above 60%, or does deceleration continue? Two consecutive quarters below 55% = real plateau
  2. Axsome Auvelity Q1 actual (Apr 29) — if >$100M, the upstream interception is accelerating
  3. COMP360 NDA filing progress — Compass targeting Q4 2026 submission; any acceleration shortens J&J's window
  4. Aticaprant Phase 3 readout timing — if positive, J&J has an oral MDD option to counter Auvelity
  5. Generic ketamine regulation — any FDA action on compounded ketamine would significantly impact the 3,500+ clinic ecosystem

6. Conversation Starters for J&J

  1. "COMP360 just got rolling NDA review AND a Commissioner's National Priority Voucher. That's the FDA saying psilocybin for TRD is a national priority. You have ~12 months before approval. The competitive intelligence study isn't optional anymore — you need to know how psychiatrists will position COMP360 vs. SPRAVATO before Compass's launch team does." — Urgent BD based on competitor milestone.

  2. "Your growth dropped from 67.8% to 55.2%. That could be seasonal — or it could be the first signal of Auvelity intercepting patients upstream. Auvelity is at ~$100M quarterly and growing 80%. Our tracking can tell you whether SPRAVATO referral patterns are changing — are fewer TRD patients being sent to certified centers because they responded to Auvelity?" — Competitor intelligence driving diagnostic research.

  3. "COMP360's '2 sessions, 6 months of benefit' vs. SPRAVATO's 'indefinite twice-monthly maintenance' is a messaging war J&J will need to fight. The counter-argument is REMS quality, insurance coverage, and long-term evidence. We should measure how psychiatrists frame this trade-off NOW, before COMP360 shapes the narrative." — Pre-emptive competitive positioning research.

Sources: J&J Q1 2026 (Apr 14), Compass Pathways Phase 3 results (Feb 2026) + FDA actions (Apr 2026). Axsome Q1 2026 pending (Apr 29). BMS Q1 2026 (Apr 24).

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
